| 2ndRegional cup first  = [[2003 East Asian Football Championship|2003]]

| 3rdRegional name       = [[FIFA ASEAN Cup]]
| 3rdRegional cup apps   = 1
| 3rdRegional cup first  = [[2026 FIFA ASEAN Cup|2026]]
| 3rdRegional cup best   = ''TBD'' (Division 2, [[2026 FIFA ASEAN Cup|2026]])
| medaltemplates         = 
{{MedalSport|Men's [[Association football|football]]}}
{{MedalCompetition | [[AFC Asian Cup]]}}
{{MedalBronze|[[1956 AFC Asian Cup|1956 Hong Kong]]|[[1956 AFC Asian Cup squads|Team]]}}
}}

The '''Hong Kong football representative team''' ({{zh|c=香港足球代表隊|cy=Hēunggóng jūkkàuh doihbíu deuih}}; recognised as '''Hong Kong, China''' by [[FIFA]]) represents [[Hong Kong]] in international [[association football|football]] and is controlled by the [[Football Association of Hong Kong, China]], the governing body for [[football in Hong Kong]].

Hong Kong had never qualified for the [[FIFA World Cup]]. They hosted the first [[AFC Asian Cup]] in 1956 and won third place, also reaching the semifinals in 1964. Hong Kong did not qualify for another AFC tournament until [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023]]. They have also qualified for the [[EAFF E-1 Football Championship]] five times, in 2003, 2010, 2019, 2022 and 2025.

Hong Kong would also host the Division 2 tournament of the inaugural [[FIFA ASEAN Cup]] in 2026.

==History==
===Establishment and pre-WWII era===
Before Hong Kong became a member of FIFA in 1954, Hong Kong began playing in the [[Hong Kong–Macau Interport]] tournament in 1937,<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.hkfa.com/news_details/5947|script-title=zh:漫談港澳埠際賽|title=maan6 taam4 gong2 ou3 fau6 zai3 coi3|language=zh-hk|publisher=HKFA|date=15 June 2008|access-date=15 June 2008|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160307153745/http://www.hkfa.com/news_details/5947|archive-date=7 March 2016}}</ref> which was one of the oldest competitions co-held by Hong Kong as well as continuously played. There were other interport tournaments in the past, such as the Shanghai-Hong Kong Interport which was first held in 1908.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.hkfa.com/news_details/8083|script-title=zh:滬港盃回顧 (一)|title=wu6 gong2 bui1 wui4 gu3 (jat1)|date=8 November 2010|access-date=11 May 2018|publisher=HKFA|language=zh-hk}}</ref> At that time the team was composed of ethnic Chinese as well as western expatriates, as in the 1935 and 1937 edition of Shanghai-Hong Kong Interport.<ref>{{cite news|title=gong2 wu6 fau6 zai3 zuk1 kau4 coi3|script-title=zh:港滬埠際足球賽|date=28 January 1935|work=The Kung Sheung Evening News|location=Hong Kong|via=Hong Kong Public Libraries MMIS|language=zh-hk}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|title=Colony soccer team favoured, but Shanghai are dangerous. Fung King Cheong must succeed, will Wilson find form?|date=10 February 1937|work=[[The China Mail]]|location=Hong Kong|via=Hong Kong Public Libraries MMIS}}</ref> There was another Interport tournament against Saigon.<ref>{{cite news|title=san1 ceon1 gaai1 zit3 zuk1 kau4 daai6 coi3 hoeng1 gong2 deoi3 kong3 sai1 gung3|script-title=zh:新春佳節足球大賽香港對抗西貢|date=29 January 1949|work=The Kung Sheung Evening News|location=Hong Kong|via=Hong Kong Public Libraries|language=zh}}</ref> The aforementioned Macau, Shanghai and Saigon were not a member of FIFA nor a sovereign nation at that time, with Hong Kong and Macau only having joined FIFA in 1954 and 1978 respectively. 

The [[China national football team|China national team]] that participated in [[Football at the 1936 Summer Olympics|1936]] and [[Football at the 1948 Summer Olympics|1948 Summer Olympics]], were mainly composed of ethnic Chinese players from Hong Kong, most famously [[Lee Wai Tong]].<ref>{{cite news|url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/Digitised/Article/nysp19480412-1.2.33|script-title=zh:我國參加世運足球隊遴選前後內幕六月上旬經星將作戰兩場|date=12 April 1948|access-date=9 September 2017|work=Nanyang Siang Pau|location=Singapore|via=Singapore National Library|title=NewspaperSG – Terms and Conditions}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|url=http://eresources.nlb.gov.sg/newspapers/Digitised/Article/nysp19480518-1.2.22.1|script-title=zh:世運足球隊一行十九人昨日下午殺抵本土容領隊發表出國戰况及出席世運日期並謂馮景祥張金海日內動程來星集隊|date=18 May 1948|access-date=9 September 2017|work=Nanyang Siang Pau|location=Singapore|via=Singapore National Library|title=NewspaperSG – Terms and Conditions}}</ref> After WWII, a number of Shanghai-based players began representing Hong Kong, such as [[Chang King Hai]] and [[Hsu King Shing]].

===FIFA member (1954–present)===
The [[Hong Kong Football Association|Hong Kong FA]] became a member of [[FIFA]] and the [[Asian Football Confederation]] in 1954. Since then Hong Kong played their first FIFA-recognised international match against other countries. The HKFA also sent a [[scratch team]] for [[1957 Merdeka Tournament]], which was composed of players from [[Eastern Sports Club|Eastern]] due to their proximity, plus few players from other clubs. The club was having a pre-season tour in South Asia, thus the HKFA invited the club to represent Hong Kong. However, some of the players were in fact ineligible to play for Hong Kong, as they were ROC (Taiwan) international players.<ref>{{cite book|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=ATWrCgAAQBAJ|script-title=zh:足球王國：戰後初期的香港足球|last=Lee|first=John C.W.|language=zh-hk|publisher=[[Joint Publishing]]|location=Hong Kong|date=2015|access-date=18 December 2017|isbn=9789620437823|page=72|via=Google Books preview|url-access=limited}}</ref><ref>{{cite news|script-title=zh:足總首次執委會議 渣甸東華均獲陞甲組 足聯抗議另開會研討 參加馬來亞獨立賽擬請東方代表|newspaper=Wah Kiu Yat Po|date=14 August 1957|page=8|language=zh-hk}}</ref>

Hong Kong qualified for three of the first four editions of the [[Asian Cup]], including a third-place finish in [[1956 AFC Asian Cup|the 1956 edition]] as host. At that time, most Hong Kong players represented [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Republic of China]]; they finished third in the Asian Cup in [[1960 AFC Asian Cup|the 1960 edition]], leaving more inferior players to the proper Hong Kong team.<ref>{{cite web|url=http://www.isu.edu.tw/upload/26/4/HS_journals/Vol1-9/11.pdf|title=The Influence of Hong Kong South China|access-date=14 October 2015|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20170810183120/http://www.isu.edu.tw/upload/26/4/HS_journals/Vol1-9/11.pdf|archive-date=10 August 2017|url-status=dead}}</ref>

The 1998 World Cup Asian qualifiers was considered one of Hong Kong's darkest moments as it was hit by a match-fixing scandal that involved former Sing Tao striker Chan Tsz-Kong who was found guilty and jailed for a year after he bribed players to throw and lose a match against Thailand. Others who were involved include goalkeeper Kevin Lok Kar-Win, defenders Chan Chi-Keung and Lau Chi Yuen and striker Wai Kwan-Lung.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/football-eight-charged-over-betting-scam-1190302.html|title=Football: Eight charged over betting scam|date=23 October 2011|work=The Independent|access-date=15 October 2021}}</ref>

====Football fever in 2015====
In 2015, a short football fever appeared during the [[2018 FIFA World Cup qualification]] matches in Hong Kong under the guidance of [[Kim Pan-gon|Kim Pan Gon]], as Hong Kong had been drawn into the same group with their fierce rival, [[China national football team|China]]. Due to the tensions built up from the Hong Kong–Mainland China conflict, many local citizens became interested in this year's campaign; all four home matches were recorded as a sellout. Hong Kong ended the campaign with 4 victories against [[Bhutan national football team|Bhutan]] and [[Maldives national football team|Maldives]], 2 scoreless draws against [[China national football team|China]], and 2 losses against [[Qatar national football team|Qatar]].

====Post-Kim era====
In late 2018, after the sudden departure of [[Kim Pan-gon]], English coach [[Gary White (footballer)|Gary White]] was hired as the new head coach in which under his guidance, he helped Hong Kong secure qualification for their third appearance at the EAFF E-1 Football Championship finals after a narrow win against [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]], a draw against [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]] and a heavy win against [[Mongolia national football team|Mongolia]]. Shortly afterwards, White departed from the role.

In April 2019, Hong Kong appointed Finnish coach [[Mixu Paatelainen]] as the new head coach of the national football team in time for the [[2022 FIFA World Cup qualifiers]] and the [[2019 EAFF E-1 Football Championship]] tournament. His first game in charge ended in a disappointing and surprising loss  at home during friendly international against [[Chinese Taipei national football team|Chinese Taipei]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=Bennett|first=Ben|date=12 June 2019|title=Hong Kong fall to disappointing defeat to Chinese Taipei – offside.hk|url=https://www.offside.hk/hkteam/hong-kong-fall-to-disappointing-defeat-to-chinese-taipei/|access-date=29 May 2024|language=en-US}}</ref> After a run of poor performances throughout the World Cup qualifiers and the 2019 EAFF E-1 Football Championship, Mixu Paatelainen's contract was not renewed.

====Return to the Asian Cup====
[[File:HK_Asian_Cup_group_photo_2.jpg|thumb|right|297x297px|Hong Kong's [[2023 AFC Asian Cup|2023 Asian Cup]] team.]]{{See also|2023 AFC Asian Cup Group C}}
On 13 December 2021, Norwegian coach [[Jørn Andersen]] who formerly guided [[North Korea national football team|North Korea]] was named as the new head coach succeeding [[Mixu Paatelainen]] in preparation for the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup qualification – third round|third round of qualification of the 2023 AFC Asian Cup]].<ref>{{Cite web|last=Standard|first=The|title=Former North Korea coach Andersen takes over Hong Kong&nbsp;national football team|url=https://www.thestandard.com.hk/breaking-news/section/8/184736/Former-North-Korea-coach-Andersen-takes-over-Hong-Kong%C2%A0national-football-team|access-date=27 March 2024|website=The Standard|language=en}}</ref> He guided Hong Kong to qualify for the [[2023 AFC Asian Cup]] by beating [[Afghanistan national football team|Afghanistan]] 2–1 and [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]] 3–0, reaching the final tournament after a 55-year absence.<ref>{{Cite web|date=14 June 2022|title=Hong Kong can dare to dream after reaching Asian Cup for first time since 1968|url=https://www.scmp.com/sport/football/article/3181616/hong-kong-qualify-asian-cup-first-time-1968-philippines-blow-chance|access-date=27 March 2024|website=South China Morning Post|language=en}}</ref> 

On 15 January 2024, Hong Kong then kicked off their [[2023 AFC Asian Cup Group C|2023 Asian Cup campaign]] against [[United Arab Emirates national football team|UAE]] in which they lost 3–1 in the opening match where [[Chan Siu Kwan]] scored the 1,000th goal in the AFC Asian Cup history.<ref>{{Cite web|title=Philip Chan scores 1000th goal of AFC Asian Cup|url=https://www.the-afc.com/en/national/afc_asian_cup/news/philip_chan_scores_1000th_goal_of_afc_asian_cup.html|access-date=27 March 2024|website=the-AFC|language=en}}</ref> They eventually finished the campaign at the bottom of their group after losses against [[Iran national football team|Iran]] (0–1) and [[Palestine national football team|Palestine]] (0–3).

====Post-Asian Cup times====
On 29 May 2024, Jørn Andersen announced his resignation as head coach of the Hong Kong national team after over 2 years in charge.<ref>{{Cite web|date=29 May 2024|title=香港隊 辭任主教練 安帥：收到冇得拒絕嘅邀請|url=https://hk.on.cc/hk/bkn/cnt/sport/20240529/bkn-20240529122118200-0529_00882_001.html|access-date=29 May 2024|website=on.cc東網|language=zh-hk}}</ref> On 28 August 2024, English manager [[Ashley Westwood (footballer, born 1976)|Ashley Westwood]] was appointed as the new head coach of the Hong Kong national football team, succeeding Jørn Andersen.<ref>{{Cite web|date=28 August 2024|title=港足｜韋斯活任新帥信能帶領進步 足總得知過去任期短：看的是能力|url=https://www.sportsroad.hk/archives/507575?fbclid=IwY2xjawE70XVleHRuA2FlbQIxMAABHWaNYC95iSAFNoPPBJCU_eZnoa7vzBNWE-9uQCGu9xkCHDxZjJNLcTT9yA_aem_z9OH1nVzwV6Wh-Ybm5OOwQ|access-date=28 August 2024|website=sportsroad.hk|language=zh-hk}}</ref> In September 2024, Hong Kong travelled to Fiji and played two friendly matches against Oceania countries, [[Solomon Islands national football team|Solomon Islands]] and [[Fiji national football team|Fiji]]. In October 2024, the team also travelled to Europe for the first time where they played against [[Liechtenstein national football team|Liechtenstein]]. On 8 December 2024, [[Yapp Hung Fai]] became the first-ever Hong Kong player to reach 100 international caps against [[Mongolia national football team|Mongolia]] during the [[2025 EAFF E-1 Football Championship|2025 EAFF E-1 Football Championship Preliminary Round]].

====Kai Tak fever in 2025====
On 10 June 2025, Hong Kong played their [[2027 AFC Asian Cup qualification]] home game against [[India national football team|India]] at the [[Kai Tak Sports Park|Kai Tak Stadium]] for the first time, which resulted in a 1–0 win for the hosts in the stadium's maiden official football match.<ref>{{Cite web |last=TVB News |first=無綫新聞 |title=Team HK thumped India at Kai Tak Sports Park {{!}} 無綫新聞TVB News |url=https://news.tvb.com/tc/pearlnews/68485d5987d260ae396da5bd/TVB%20News-Team-HK-thumped-India-at-Kai-Tak-Sports-Park |access-date=2025-06-11 |website=無綫新聞 TVB News |language=zh-HK}}</ref> All tickets were sold out and a new attendance record was set for a Hong Kong football match with 42,570 spectators.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Hong Kong, China beat India 1-0 in Kai Tak debut - RTHK |url=https://news.rthk.hk/rthk/en/component/k2/1808456-20250610.htm? |access-date=2025-06-11 |website=news.rthk.hk |language=en-gb}}</ref><ref>{{Cite web |title=Record-breaking audience cheers Hong Kong to 1-0 victory over India |url=https://www.thestandard.com.hk/hong-kong-news/article/304429/Record-breaking-audience-cheers-Hong-Kong-to-1-0-victory-over-India |access-date=2025-06-11 |website=www.thestandard.com.hk |language=en}}</ref>

On 18 November, Hong Kong failed to qualify for the [[2027 AFC Asian Cup]] after succumbing to a 1–2 loss against [[Singapore national football team|Singapore]] in front of a record crowd of 47,762. On 24 November, [[Ashley Westwood (footballer, born 1976)|Ashley Westwood]] stepped down from the role.<ref>{{Cite web |last=Limbu |first=byPrashan |title=Ashley Westwood out as Hong Kong head coach after Asian Cup heartbreak |url=https://www.thestandard.com.hk/hong-kong-news/article/317658/Ashley-Westwood-out-as-Hong-Kong-head-coach-after-Asian-Cup-heartbreak |access-date=2025-11-24 |website=www.thestandard.com.hk |language=en}}</ref>

==Team image==
===Kits===
The national team's home kit is typically a red shirt, red shorts, and red or white socks, while the away kit features white shirts, white shorts, and red or white socks.

====Kit suppliers====
{| class="wikitable"
|-
!Kit supplier 
!Period 
|-
|{{flagdeco|Germany}} [[Puma (brand)|Puma]]
|1970s–1998
|-
|{{flagdeco|Germany}} [[Adidas (brand)|Adidas]]
|1998
|-
|{{flagdeco|Italy}} [[Diadora]]
|2000–2005
|-
|{{flagdeco|Germany}} [[Adidas]]
|2005–2011
|-
|{{flagdeco|USA}} [[Nike, Inc.|Nike]]
|2011–present 
|}

===Crest===
The crest of the Hong Kong national football team features a [[Chinese dragon]]. This logo has consistently been used as the team's emblem. The HKFA emblem was not used on jerseys until 31 May 2011, HKFA debuted current emblem for the national team.

===Home stadiums===
{{See also|Hong Kong Stadium|Mong Kok Stadium|Kai Tak Sports Park|Jockey Club HKFA Football Training Centre}}
The team's primary stadium are [[Hong Kong Stadium]] and [[Kai Tak Sports Park]]. For selected friendly matches and minor qualification matches, the Hong Kong team plays most often at the [[Mong Kok Stadium]] in Kowloon.<ref>{{cite news|url=http://orientaldaily.on.cc/cnt/sport/20180219/index.html|script-title=zh:旺角場今午雙喜臨門 港隊港聯同球迷賀歲|date=19 February 2018|access-date=14 November 2018|newspaper=Oriental Daily|location=Hong Kong|language=zh-hk}}</ref>

The [[Jockey Club HKFA Football Training Centre]] is currently the main training ground for the Hong Kong national and youth teams.

===Rivalries===

====China====
{{main|China–Hong Kong football rivalry}}

{{see also|1985 China v Hong Kong football match|Hong Kong–Mainland China conflict}}

Hong Kong maintains a specific rivalry with [[China national football team|China]]. The rivalry began in 1978 and on 19 May 1985, Hong Kong produced a shock 2–1 upset in Beijing in the [[Football World Cup 1986 (qualification)|1986 World Cup qualifying]] game, leading to unrest by Chinese supporters.<ref>{{cite web|url=https://supchina.com/2020/08/26/when-hong-kong-football-sparked-a-riot-in-beijing/|title=The 'May 19 Incident': When Hong Kong football sparked a riot in Beijing|date=26 August 2020|access-date=5 November 2020|archive-date=21 May 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220521104751/https://supchina.com/2020/08/26/when-hong-kong-football-sparked-a-riot-in-beijing/|url-status=dead}}</ref> 

Since then, China was unbeaten against to Hong Kong but the rivalry continues and even got heated up since the conflict between Hong Kong and China in the 2010s.<ref>{{cite news|url=https://www.highbeam.com/doc/1P1-88092608.html|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20161008195838/https://www.highbeam.com/doc/1P1-88092608.html|url-status=dead|archive-date=8 October 2016|title=Hong Kong and China to meet in one group|agency=Associated Press|url-access=subscription|via=[[HighBeam]]|access-date=16 September 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.scmp.com/sport/hong-kong/article/1855037/china-v-hong-kong-world-cup-qualifier-live-blog|title=Against all odds, Hong Kong hold China to 0-0 draw in World Cup qualifier|work=South China Morning Post|date=3 September 2015|access-date=16 September 2016}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.scmp.com/sport/hong-kong/article/3042652/eaff-china-beat-hong-kong-fans-boo-chinese-anthem-amid-protest|title=EAFF: China beat Hong Kong as fans boo Chinese anthem amid protest flags in South Korea|work=South China Morning Post|date=18 December 2019|access-date=18 December 2019}}</ref>

On 1 January 2024, Hong Kong defeated China 2–1 in a closed door FIFA international friendly, marking their first victory in 29 years.<ref>{{Cite web|date=2 January 2024|title=Andersen hails Hong Kong's 'aggression' in historic win over China|url=https://www.scmp.com/sport/football/article/3246926/hong-kongs-footballers-beat-china-first-time-29-years|access-date=2 January 2024|website=South China Morning Post|language=en}}</ref><ref>{{Cite news |last=Hawkins |first=Amy |date=2 January 2024 |title=Hong Kong beat China at football for first time in 29 years |url=https://www.theguardian.com/world/2024/jan/02/hong-kong-beat-china-at-football-for-first-time-in-29-years |access-date=27 March 2024 |work=The Guardian |language=en-GB |issn=0261-3077}}</ref>

====Macau====
{{See also|Hong Kong–Macau Interport}}
The [[Hong Kong–Macau Interport|Hong Kong–Macau rivalry]] has been contested by [[Hong Kong Football Association]] and [[Macau Football Association]] since 1937.

==Results and fixtures==
{{Main|Hong Kong national football team results (2020s)}}

{{See also|2025–26 in Hong Kong football}}
The following is a list of match results in the last 12 months, as well as any future matches that have been scheduled.

| referee    = Torphong Somsing ([[Football Association of Thailand|Thailand]])
| result     = W
}}

<small>Last updated: [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]] 2–0 Hong Kong , 9 June 2026. Statistics include international "A" matches only.</small>
{{notelist}}

==Players==
===Current squad===
The following 22 players have been named in the final squad for the friendly matches against [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]] on 9 June 2026.<ref>{{Cite web |title=中國香港足球總會 |url=https://www.hkfa.com//en/news/announcements/22504/detail |access-date=2026-05-27 |website=中國香港足球總會 |language=en-US}}</ref>

''Caps and goals as of 9 June 2026 after the match against [[Cambodia national football team|Cambodia]].''

{{nat fs break}}
<sup>PRE</sup> Preliminary squad.<br>
<sup>INJ</sup> Player withdrew from the squad due to an injury<br>
<sup>WD</sup> Player withdrew from the squad.<br>
<sup>RET</sup> Player retired from international football
{{nat fs end}}

==History of naturalised players==
During the 1950s, Arthur Santos who is of British-Portuguese nationality (whose son [[Leslie Santos|Leslie]] was a former Hong Kong international footballer) became the first naturalised player to represent the Hong Kong national football team who was then followed by another fellow Portuguese-born player named JH Toleido.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|title=歸化交叉點——歸化香港，請居滿七年|url=https://football.fanpiece.com/trashtalking-soccer/%E6%AD%B8%E5%8C%96%E4%BA%A4%E5%8F%89%E9%BB%9E-%E6%AD%B8%E5%8C%96%E9%A6%99%E6%B8%AF-%E8%AB%8B%E5%B1%85%E6%BB%BF%E4%B8%83%E5%B9%B4-c1170630.html|access-date=2 February 2024|website=FanPiece|language=zh-Hant-HK}}</ref>

In the 1960s, there were a couple more foreign players who had represented the Hong Kong national football team whilst some were working within the national service at the time. These include British players from T. Watson, Evans, [[Ken Wallis (bowls)|Ken Wallis]] who went on to represent Hong Kong during the lawn bowls event during the [[1990 Commonwealth Games|1990]] and [[1994 Commonwealth Games]] as well as Australian-born Pete McClaren and Scottish-born [[Charlie Wright]].<ref name=":0" /> 

In the late 1970s, Scottish-born players [[Derek Currie]], Dave Anderson and Hugh McCrory all became eligible to represent the Hong Kong national football team in which Currie and Anderson took part during the 1979 Asian Cup qualifiers whilst McCrory took part during the 1982 World Cup qualifiers.

There were at least a couple more naturalised players who went on to represent Hong Kong throughout the 90's which include Bosnian-born [[Anto Grabo]] along with fellow English-born players Mark Grainger, [[John Moore (footballer, born 1966)|John Moore]] and most notably [[Dale Tempest]]. [[Sung Lin Yung]] became the first mainland born player to represent Hong Kong during the 1998 World Cup Asian qualifiers having resided for more than two years under FIFA eligibility rules unlike foreign born players that would usually require at least seven years.

In the 2000s, a couple of African and Brazilian-born players were introduced went through the naturalisation process having met the residential criteria. Nigerian-born [[Lawrence Akandu]] obtained his Hong Kong citizenship in which he played for the national team during the [[2003 East Asian Football Championship|2003 East Asia Cup]] finals where he scored a goal in a loss against South Korea. He was soon followed by Cameroon-born [[Guy Gerard Ambassa]] who obtained his permanent residential status in 2005 along with another fellow Nigerian-born player named [[Colly Ezeh]] and Brazilian-born [[Cristiano Cordeiro]] in which both of whom earned international caps during the [[2007 AFC Asian Cup qualification|2007 AFC Asian Cup]] qualifiers. Cordeiro was also the first non-Chinese captain in the history of the Hong Kong team during the 2008 East Asia Cup preliminary stages. Despite having played for the national team during the 2009 edition of the [[Guangdong–Hong Kong Cup|Guangdong-Hong Kong Cup]], Cameroonian-born [[Julius Akosah]] attempted to apply for a HKSAR passport, however his application was unsuccessful.

During the mid 2010s, there had been an increase of naturalised players being used to represent the national team in which former head coach [[Kim Pan-gon]] stated that he needed to pick his best players regardless of their origin in preparation during the 2018 World Cup qualifiers.<ref>{{Cite web|last=Chan|first=Lester|date=29 September 2022|title=Obsession with naturalized players overlooks impending crisis – offside.hk|url=https://www.offside.hk/hkteam/obsession-with-naturalized-players-overlooks-impending-crisis/|access-date=2 February 2024|language=en-US}}</ref> 

These include the likes of European-born players [[Clement Benhaddouche]], [[Manuel Bleda]], [[Dani Cancela]], [[Jaimes McKee]], [[Raphaël Merkies]], [[Fernando Recio]], [[Andy Russell (footballer, born 1987)|Andy Russell]], [[Jack Sealy]], [[Jordi Tarrés (footballer)|Jordi Tarrés]] and [[Sean Tse]], Brazilian-born players [[Everton Camargo]], [[Paulo César (footballer, born 1986)|Paulo César]], [[Clayton (footballer, born 1988)|Clayton]], [[Dudu (footballer, born 17 April 1990)|Dudu]], [[Diego Eli]], [[Fernando (footballer, born November 1986)|Fernando]], [[Giovane (footballer, born 1982)|Giovane]], [[Hélio (footballer)|Helio]], [[Itaparica (footballer)|Itaparica]], [[Juninho (footballer, born December 1990)|Juninho]], [[Roberto Júnior]], [[Tomas Maronesi]], [[Paulinho (footballer, born January 1983)|Paulinho]], [[Stefan Pereira]], and [[Sandro (footballer, born March 1987)|Sandro]], Asian-born players [[Jahangir Khan (footballer)|Jahangir Khan]] and [[Yuto Nakamura]], and African-born players from [[Wisdom Fofo Agbo]], [[Alex Tayo Akande|Alex Akande]], [[Christian Annan]], [[Mahama Awal]], [[Festus Baise]], [[Godfred Karikari]], [[Jean-Jacques Kilama]], and [[Paul Ngue]].

In addition to [[Sung Lin Yung]], several other mainland born players went on to represent Hong Kong from past to present which include [[Bai He]], [[Chao Pengfei]], [[Deng Jinghuang]], Feng Jizhi, [[Gao Wen]], [[Li Haiqiang]], [[Liu Quankun]], [[Huang Yang]], [[Ju Yingzhi]], [[Wang Zhenpeng (footballer)|Wang Zhenpeng]], [[Wei Zhao (footballer)|Wei Zhao]], Xiao Guoji, [[Xu Deshuai]], [[Ye Jia]], and [[Zhang Chunhui]].<ref>{{Cite web|title=歸化交叉點——國援上馬又如何？|url=https://football.fanpiece.com/trashtalking-soccer/%E6%AD%B8%E5%8C%96%E4%BA%A4%E5%8F%89%E9%BB%9E-%E5%9C%8B%E6%8F%B4%E4%B8%8A%E9%A6%AC%E5%8F%88%E5%A6%82%E4%BD%95-c1193017.html|access-date=3 February 2024|website=FanPiece|language=zh-Hant-HK}}</ref>

==Records==
{{Updated|9 June 2026}}<ref>{{cite web|last1=Mamrud|first1=Roberto|title=Hongkong – Record International Players|url=https://www.rsssf.org/miscellaneous/hk-recintlp.html|publisher=Rec.Sport.Soccer Statistics Foundation}}</ref>
:''Players in '''bold''' are still active with Hong Kong.''

==Captains==
This list only records the players who were named as Hong Kong captain in official international competitions. First-choice captains always go first.
[[File:Huang Yang.JPG|thumb|right|[[Huang Yang]] was the captain of Hong Kong from 2018 to 2023.<ref name="facebook">{{cite web|url=https://www.facebook.com/hkleague/posts/2194909453852657|archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/iarchive/facebook/484506791559607/2194909453852657|archive-date=26 February 2022|url-access=limited|script-title=zh:毋懼朝鮮排名高 主教練韋特：有信心取勝|title=mou4 geoi6 ciu4 sin1 paai4 ming4 gou1 zyu2 gaau3 lin6 wai4 dak6: jau5 seon3 sam1 ceoi2 sing3|language=zh-hk|publisher=HKFA Facebook|date=12 November 2018|access-date=14 November 2018}}{{cbignore}}</ref>]]

!Year
!Result
!Position
!width=30|{{Tooltip|Pld|Matches Played}}
!width=30|{{Tooltip|W|Matches won}}
!width=30|{{Tooltip|D*|Drawn}}
!width=30|{{Tooltip|L|Matches lost}}
!width=30|{{Tooltip|GF|Goals for}}
!width=30|{{Tooltip|GA|Goals against}}
|-
|{{flagicon|IND}} [[Football at the 1951 Asian Games|1951]]||colspan=8|''Did not enter''
|-
|{{flagicon|PHL|1936}} [[Football at the 1954 Asian Games|1954]]||First round||5||2||1||1||0||7||5
|-
|{{flagicon|JPN|1870}} [[Football at the 1958 Asian Games|1958]]||Quarter-finals||7||3||2||0||1||8||6
|-
|{{flagicon|IDN}} [[Football at the 1962 Asian Games|1962]] to {{flagicon|KOR}} [[Football at the 1986 Asian Games|1986]]||colspan=8|''Did not enter''
|-
|{{flagicon|CHN}} [[Football at the 1990 Asian Games|1990]]||First round||9||3||1||0||2||3||4
|-
|{{flagicon|JPN|1870}} [[Football at the 1994 Asian Games|1994]]||First round||12||4||1||0||3||6||8
|-
|{{flagicon|THA}} [[Football at the 1998 Asian Games|1998]]||First round||22||2||0||0||2||0||11
|-
|{{flagicon|KOR}} [[Football at the 2002 Asian Games – Men's tournament|2002]] to present
|colspan=8|''See [[Hong Kong national under-23 football team#Asian Games record|Hong Kong national U-23 team]]''
|-
!'''Total†'''||colspan=2|'''Quarter-finals'''||14||5||1||8||24||34
|}
<small>† Excluding 1998 onwards</small>
{{col-break|align=right}}
{| class="wikitable" style="text-align: center;font-size:90%;"
|-
!colspan=4|Asian Games history
|-
!Year!!Round!!Score!!Result
|-
|rowspan=2|{{flagicon|PHL|1936}} [[Football at the 1954 Asian Games|1954]]
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1910}} '''3–3''' {{fb|KOR|1949}}||bgcolor=#FFFFBB|Draw
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1910}} '''4–2''' {{fb|AFG|1930}}||bgcolor=#BBF3BB|Win
|-
|rowspan=3|{{flagicon|JPN}} [[Football at the 1958 Asian Games|1958]]
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''4–1''' {{fb|PHI|1936}}||bgcolor=#BBF3BB|Win
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''2–0''' {{fb|JPN|1870}}||bgcolor=#BBF3BB|Win
|-
|Quarter-finals||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''2–5''' {{fb|IND}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|rowspan=3|{{flagicon|CHN}} [[Football at the 1990 Asian Games|1990]]
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''1–2''' {{fb|KUW}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''0–2''' {{fb|THA}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''2–0''' {{fb|YEM}}||bgcolor=#BBF3BB|Win
|-
|rowspan=4|{{flagicon|JPN}} [[Football at the 1994 Asian Games|1994]]
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''3–4''' {{fb|MAS}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''2–1''' {{fb|THA}}||bgcolor=#BBF3BB|Win
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''0–1''' {{fb|UZB}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG|1959}} '''1–2''' {{fb|KSA}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|rowspan=4|{{flagicon|THA}} [[Football at the 1998 Asian Games|1998]]
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG}} '''0–6''' {{fb|OMA}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|-
|First round||align="left"|{{fb|HKG}} '''0–5''' {{fb|THA}}||bgcolor=#FFBBBB|Loss
|}
{{col-end}}

==Honours==
<!-- DO NOT ADD UNOFFICIAL OR FRIENDLY COMPETITIONS IN THE SUMMARY TABLE FOR OFFICIAL HONOURS ONLY -->
===Continental===
* '''[[AFC Asian Cup]]'''
** {{Bronze3}}  Third place (1): [[1956 AFC Asian Cup|1956]]

===Friendly===
* '''[[Long Teng Cup]]''': 
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (2)''': [[2010 Long Teng Cup|2010]], [[2011 Long Teng Cup|2011]]
* '''Tri-Nations Series'''
** {{Gold1}} '''Champions (1)''': 2025
* '''[[King's Cup (Thailand)|King's Cup]]'''
** {{Bronze3}} Third place (1): [[2025 King's Cup|2025]]

===Awards===
* '''EAFF Championship Fair Play Award (1)''': [[2010 East Asian Football Championship|2010]]

===Summary===
Only official honours are included, according to ''FIFA statutes'' (competitions organized/recognized by [[FIFA]] or an affiliated confederation).
